questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When viewed from an inertial frame of reference, a particle of mass $m$ is seen travelling in a circle of radius $r$ at a uniform speed $v$. What is the centrifugal force acting on the particle?
options: $\dfrac{mv^2}{r}$ along the tangent passing through the particle
questions.solution:
Centrifugal force is a pseudo force which is experienced only in a non-inertial (rotating) frame of reference.

When viewed from an inertial frame of reference, no pseudo forces are present. The particle executes circular motion solely due to a real centripetal force of magnitude $\dfrac{mv^2}{r}$ directed towards the centre.

Thus, the centrifugal force acting on the particle in an inertial frame is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A coin resting on a rotating turntable just begins to slip when positioned $4 \text{ cm}$ from the centre. If the turntable's angular velocity is doubled, the coin will just slip at a distance of
questions.solution:
The condition for the coin to just slip on the rotating turntable is that the required centripetal force is provided by the maximum static friction.

$m r \omega^2 = \mu m g$

$r = \dfrac{\mu g}{\omega^2}$

This implies that $r \propto \dfrac{1}{\omega^2}$.

Let the initial angular velocity be $\omega_1 = \omega$ and the initial distance be $r_1 = 4 \text{ cm}$.

When the angular velocity is doubled, $\omega_2 = 2\omega$.

Using the proportionality $r_1 \omega_1^2 = r_2 \omega_2^2$, we get:

$4 \times \omega^2 = r_2 \times (2\omega)^2$

$4 \omega^2 = r_2 \times 4\omega^2$

$r_2 = 1 \text{ cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A motorcycle travels across an overbridge of radius $R$. The driver keeps the speed constant. While the motorcycle is ascending the overbridge, the normal force acting on it
questions.solution:
Let $\theta$ be the angle made by the radius vector of the motorcycle with the vertical.

The forces acting on the motorcycle along the radial direction are the component of weight $mg \cos\theta$ towards the center and the normal reaction $N$ away from the center.

The net force provides the necessary centripetal acceleration:

$mg \cos\theta - N = \dfrac{mv^2}{R}$

$N = mg \cos\theta - \dfrac{mv^2}{R}$

While the motorcycle is ascending the overbridge, it moves towards the highest point, so the angle $\theta$ decreases.

As $\theta$ decreases, the value of $\cos\theta$ increases.

Since the speed $v$ is constant, the term $\dfrac{mv^2}{R}$ remains constant.

Therefore, the normal force $N$ increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three identical cars, $A$, $B$, and $C$, travel at the same speed across three bridges. Car $A$ drives on a flat bridge, car $B$ on a bridge that is convex upward, and car $C$ on a bridge that is concave upward. Let $F_A$, $F_B$, and $F_C$ denote the normal forces exerted by the cars on the bridges when they are at the center of the bridges. Which of the following statements is correct?
options: $F_B$ is the largest of the three forces
$F_A$ is the largest of the three forces
$F_C$ is the largest of the three forces
questions.solution:
For car $A$ moving on a flat bridge, the vertical acceleration is zero. The normal force is given by $F_A = mg$.

For car $B$ moving on a convex upward bridge, the centripetal acceleration is directed downwards towards the center of curvature. The equation of motion is $mg - F_B = \dfrac{mv^2}{R}$, which gives $F_B = mg - \dfrac{mv^2}{R}$.

For car $C$ moving on a concave upward bridge, the centripetal acceleration is directed upwards towards the center of curvature. The equation of motion is $F_C - mg = \dfrac{mv^2}{R}$, which gives $F_C = mg + \dfrac{mv^2}{R}$.

Comparing the three normal forces, we get $F_C > F_A > F_B$.

Therefore, $F_C$ is the largest of the three forces.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Along the equator, a train $A$ travels from east to west while a second train $B$ of identical mass travels from west to east at an equal speed. Train $A$ exerts a force $F_1$ on the track, whereas train $B$ exerts a force $F_2$ on the track.
options: The given information is insufficient to establish a relation between $F_1$ and $F_2$
questions.solution:
Let $v$ be the speed of the trains relative to the Earth, $\omega$ be the angular velocity of the Earth, and $R$ be the radius of the Earth.

The Earth rotates from west to east. The absolute speed of a point on the equator is $\omega R$ from west to east.

Train A travels from east to west. Its absolute speed is $v_A = \omega R - v$.

Train B travels from west to east. Its absolute speed is $v_B = \omega R + v$.

The normal force $N$ exerted by the track on the train provides the necessary centripetal force along with gravity. The equation of motion is $mg - N = \dfrac{m v_{abs}^2}{R}$, which gives $N = m \left( g - \dfrac{v_{abs}^2}{R} \right)$.

For train A, $F_1 = m \left( g - \dfrac{(\omega R - v)^2}{R} \right)$.

For train B, $F_2 = m \left( g - \dfrac{(\omega R + v)^2}{R} \right)$.

Since $(\omega R + v)^2 > (\omega R - v)^2$, it follows that $F_1 > F_2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a particle travels in a circular path at a uniform speed,
options: both its velocity and acceleration change
its acceleration is constant but its velocity changes
its velocity and acceleration are both constant
its velocity is constant but its acceleration changes
questions.solution:
When a particle moves in a circular path with uniform speed, its direction of motion changes continuously at every point.

Since velocity is a vector quantity, a change in direction means the velocity changes continuously.

The acceleration of the particle is centripetal, which is always directed towards the center of the circular path.

As the particle moves, the direction towards the center changes continuously, meaning the acceleration vector also changes continuously.

Therefore, both velocity and acceleration change.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two cars with masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ travel in circles of radii $r_1$ and $r_2$, respectively. If they complete one circle in equal time, the ratio of their angular speeds $\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2}$ is
questions.solution:
The time taken to complete one circle is the time period $T$.

Given that both cars complete one circle in equal time, their time periods are equal, $T_1 = T_2$.

The angular speed is given by $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$.

Taking the ratio of the angular speeds of the two cars:

$\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{\dfrac{2\pi}{T_1}}{\dfrac{2\pi}{T_2}} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$

Since $T_1 = T_2$, we get $\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = 1$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Water contained in a bucket is whirled in a vertical circle using an attached string. The water does not fall out even when the bucket is inverted at the highest point of its trajectory. From this, we can conclude that in this position,
questions.solution:
At the highest point of the vertical circle, the forces acting on the water are its weight $mg$ acting downwards and the normal reaction $N$ exerted by the bottom of the bucket, also acting downwards.

The net downward force provides the necessary centripetal force for circular motion:

$N + mg = \dfrac{mv^2}{r}$

For the water not to fall out, it must maintain contact with the bucket, which means the normal reaction $N$ must be greater than or equal to zero, i.e., $N \ge 0$.

$\dfrac{mv^2}{r} - mg \ge 0$

$mg \le \dfrac{mv^2}{r}$

This implies that $mg$ is not greater than $\dfrac{mv^2}{r}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle is securely placed on a uniformly rotating turntable. When observed from the ground, the particle travels in a circle with a speed of $20 \text{ cm/s}$ and an acceleration of $20 \text{ cm/s}^2$. The particle is subsequently relocated to a new position, making the radius half of its initial value. Determine the new values for the speed and acceleration.
questions.solution:
For a particle on a uniformly rotating turntable, the angular velocity $\omega$ remains constant.

The initial speed is given by $v_1 = \omega r_1 = 20 \text{ cm/s}$.

The initial centripetal acceleration is given by $a_1 = \omega^2 r_1 = 20 \text{ cm/s}^2$.

When the particle is relocated such that the new radius is $r_2 = \dfrac{r_1}{2}$, the new speed $v_2$ is:

$v_2 = \omega r_2 = \omega \left(\dfrac{r_1}{2}\right) = \dfrac{v_1}{2} = \dfrac{20}{2} = 10 \text{ cm/s}$

The new acceleration $a_2$ is:

$a_2 = \omega^2 r_2 = \omega^2 \left(\dfrac{r_1}{2}\right) = \dfrac{a_1}{2} = \dfrac{20}{2} = 10 \text{ cm/s}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the earth were to stop rotating, the apparent value of $g$ on its surface would
options: increase at some locations and remain unchanged at others
questions.solution:
The apparent acceleration due to gravity at a latitude $\lambda$ on the surface of the earth is given by:

$g' = g - R\omega^2 \cos^2 \lambda$

where $R$ is the radius of the earth and $\omega$ is its angular velocity.

If the earth stops rotating, $\omega = 0$, and the apparent acceleration due to gravity becomes $g' = g$.

At the poles ($\lambda = 90^{\circ}$), $\cos \lambda = 0$, so $g' = g$ both before and after the earth stops rotating. The value of $g$ remains unchanged at the poles.

At the equator ($\lambda = 0^{\circ}$), $\cos \lambda = 1$, so $g'$ changes from $g - R\omega^2$ to $g$. The value of $g$ increases.

At all other latitudes ($0^{\circ} < \lambda < 90^{\circ}$), $g'$ increases as the subtractive term $R\omega^2 \cos^2 \lambda$ becomes zero.

Thus, the apparent value of $g$ increases at some locations and remains unchanged at others.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rod of length $L$ is pivoted at one of its ends and rotated in a horizontal plane with a uniform angular velocity. Let $T_1$ and $T_2$ represent the tensions at distances $L/4$ and $3L/4$, respectively, from the pivoted end.
options: The relationship between $T_1$ and $T_2$ depends on whether the rotation is clockwise or anticlockwise
questions.solution:
Let the mass per unit length of the rod be $\lambda$ and its angular velocity be $\omega$.

Consider an element of length $dx$ at a distance $x$ from the pivoted end. The centripetal force required for this element is $dF = (\lambda dx) \omega^2 x$.

The tension $T(x)$ at a distance $x$ from the pivot provides the necessary centripetal force for the remaining portion of the rod from $x$ to $L$.

$T(x) = \int_{x}^{L} \lambda \omega^2 x' dx' = \dfrac{\lambda \omega^2}{2} (L^2 - x^2)$

Since $T(x)$ is a decreasing function of $x$, the tension is greater at points closer to the pivot.

Given $x_1 = \dfrac{L}{4}$ and $x_2 = \dfrac{3L}{4}$, we have $x_1 < x_2$.

Therefore, $T_1 > T_2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A simple pendulum with a bob of mass $m$ hangs from the ceiling of a car involved in a stunt film shoot. The car travels upward along an inclined cliff at a speed $v$, leaps off the cliff, and lands some distance away. Suppose $R$ is the maximum height reached by the car above the cliff's top. The tension in the string while the car is airborne is
questions.solution:
When the car is airborne, it is in a state of free fall and its acceleration is equal to the acceleration due to gravity $g$ directed downwards.

In the non-inertial frame of reference of the car, a pseudo force of magnitude $mg$ acts on the pendulum bob in the upward direction.

The true gravitational force acting on the bob is $mg$ in the downward direction.

The pseudo force exactly cancels the gravitational force, making the effective acceleration due to gravity zero in the car's frame.

Since the effective gravity is zero, the bob does not exert any pull on the string. Therefore, the tension in the string is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider a simple pendulum oscillating in a vertical plane, with $\theta$ representing its angular displacement. If the mass of the bob is $m$, the tension in the string is equal to $mg\cos\theta$
questions.solution:
Let $T$ be the tension in the string, $m$ be the mass of the bob, $v$ be its velocity, and $L$ be the length of the pendulum.

The forces acting on the bob in the radial direction are the tension $T$ directed towards the pivot and the component of weight $mg\cos\theta$ directed away from the pivot.

Using Newton's second law for circular motion, the net radial force provides the necessary centripetal acceleration:

$T - mg\cos\theta = \dfrac{mv^2}{L}$

$T = mg\cos\theta + \dfrac{mv^2}{L}$

For the tension to be equal to $mg\cos\theta$, the centripetal term must be zero:

$\dfrac{mv^2}{L} = 0 \Rightarrow v = 0$

The velocity of the pendulum bob is zero only at the extreme positions of its oscillation.

Therefore, the tension in the string is equal to $mg\cos\theta$ at the extreme positions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of mass $m$ revolves in a circle of radius $a$ about the $Z$-axis at a uniform angular speed $\omega$. When observed from a reference frame rotating about the $Z$-axis with a uniform angular speed $\omega_0$, the centrifugal force acting on the particle is
questions.solution:
The centrifugal force is a pseudo force that arises when observations are made from a rotating reference frame.

The magnitude of the centrifugal force acting on a particle of mass $m$ at a perpendicular distance $r$ from the axis of rotation is given by $F_{c} = m \omega_{\text{frame}}^2 r$, where $\omega_{\text{frame}}$ is the angular speed of the rotating reference frame.

In the given problem, the reference frame rotates with an angular speed $\omega_0$ about the $Z$-axis, and the particle is at a distance $a$ from the $Z$-axis.

Therefore, the centrifugal force acting on the particle is $m \omega_0^2 a$.

The actual angular speed $\omega$ of the particle does not affect the centrifugal force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A car travels at a constant speed along a road, as shown in the figure. The normal force exerted by the road on the car is $N_A$ when the car is at point $A$, and $N_B$ when it is at point $B$.
options: the relationship between $N_A$ and $N_B$ cannot be determined from the given information
questions.solution:
At the highest point of a convex path, the net force towards the center of curvature provides the necessary centripetal force.

The equation of motion is:
$mg - N = \dfrac{mv^2}{R}$

Rearranging for the normal force $N$, we get:
$N = mg - \dfrac{mv^2}{R}$

From the given figure, the curve at point A is sharper than the curve at point B. This means the radius of curvature at A is smaller than that at B, i.e., $R_A < R_B$.

Since the car travels at a constant speed $v$, the term $\dfrac{mv^2}{R}$ is larger for point A than for point B.

Therefore, $\dfrac{mv^2}{R_A} > \dfrac{mv^2}{R_B}$

$\Rightarrow mg - \dfrac{mv^2}{R_A} < mg - \dfrac{mv^2}{R_B}$

$\Rightarrow N_A < N_B$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A stone of mass $m$ attached to a string of length $l$ is whirled in a circle, with the string's other end acting as the centre. The stone's speed is $v$. In the event that the string breaks, the stone will travel
questions.solution:
When a stone of mass $m$ is whirled in a circular path of radius $l$ with speed $v$, its velocity at any instant is directed along the tangent to the circle at that point. The centripetal force required for circular motion is provided by the tension in the string.

If the string breaks, the tension becomes zero and the centripetal force vanishes.

According to Newton's first law of motion, in the absence of any external force, the stone will continue to move in the direction of its instantaneous velocity.

Therefore, the stone will travel along a tangent to the circular path.
